title: Grouping Data (GROUP BY)
description: Create powerful sub-totals based on categories.

slides.title: The Power of Subtotals 🗃️
slides.content:
# Smashing data intelligently

`SUM()` and `COUNT()` are great, but they smash the ENTIRE table into one single number.

What if you run an international store, and you want to know the Total Sales... *per country*?

You want a list showing: USA ($5000), Canada ($3000), Mexico ($1000).

slides.title: Enter GROUP BY 🤝
slides.content:
## Organizing the smash

The **`GROUP BY`** clause tells the database to group rows together based on identical values in a certain column, and THEN calculate the aggregation for each group.

`SELECT country, SUM(revenue) FROM sales GROUP BY country;`

This groups all USA rows into one bucket, sums their revenue, outputs it, then moves to Canada, does the same, and so on!

quizData.question: What is the primary function of `GROUP BY`?

options.text: To split the database into multiple files
To organize identical data into groups, allowing aggregation functions (like SUM or COUNT) to calculate subtotals per group

quizData.explanation: GROUP BY is how you generate segmented subtotals, like counting how many users exist PER city, rather than one giant global count.

slides.title: The Golden Rule of Grouping 🚨
slides.content:
## The Number One SQL Error

If you use `GROUP BY`, your `SELECT` statement can ONLY contain two things:
1. The actual column(s) you are grouping by.
2. Aggregation functions (like COUNT, SUM).

If you group by `country`, you CANNOT say `SELECT name, country...`. The database would have no idea which name to pick, because you just smashed a thousand people into one massive USA bucket!

quizData.question: If you write `GROUP BY category`, what is the ONLY non-aggregated column you are allowed to put in your `SELECT` clause?

options.text: None, you can only use COUNT()

quizData.explanation: When you group data by category, the concept of individual row details vanishes. Therefore, you can only SELECT the category itself alongside your mathematical aggregations.

slides.title: Try it Out!
slides.content: Group the `users` table by `role`, and `COUNT` how many users are in each role.
